The preview you see in the camera is a temporary JPEG thumbnail; the actual RAW data is processed by Lightroom, Capture One, or similar software. File size is large (~20-50 MB depending on sensor).

**What RAW recovery enables:**
* Recover highlights blown in-camera by 2-3 stops (bright sky that showed as pure white in the camera preview is often fully recoverable)
* Lift shadows underexposed by 3-5 stops with acceptable noise
* Shift white balance freely after capture with no quality penalty
* Apply all corrections non-destructively and reverse them at any time

**RAW formats by drone manufacturer:**
* DJI cameras: .DNG (Adobe Digital Negative, open standard, natively supported by Lightroom)
* Autel: .DNG or proprietary .RAW
* Hasselblad (DJI Inspire + Zenmuse X7): .3FR
* Sony (on certain platforms): .ARW

**DNG advantage:** DJI's use of the open DNG standard means Lightroom Classic imports and profiles DJI files automatically, with no additional camera support packs required.

### Lightroom Classic vs. Lightroom CC

**Lightroom Classic:** Desktop application with full local file management, powerful organizational tools (folders, collections, smart collections), and the complete editing toolkit. The professional standard. Requires Adobe Creative Cloud subscription (~$10/month photography plan).

**Lightroom CC (Cloud):** Simplified cloud-first interface. Good for mobile editing and quick sharing. Less organizational depth. Better for casual use; less suited for high-volume professional work.

**Recommendation:** Lightroom Classic for any professional aerial work. The organizational tools are critical when managing thousands of images across multiple clients, locations, and dates.

### Lightroom Import Workflow

**Before importing:**
1. Copy all memory card contents to your project folder on your primary drive (follow the 3-2-1 backup rule)
2. Never import directly from a memory card — Lightroom embeds absolute file paths; a card removed later creates missing file references

**Import dialog settings:**
1. File -> Import Photos and Video (or connect card/drive and use the Import dialog)
2. Choose **Copy** (not Add) when importing from a card to move files to your organized folder
3. Set the destination folder structure: Year/Month-Date-ClientName/ (e.g., 2024/2024-03-15-MarinaBay/)
4. **File Renaming:** Rename at import using a template: YYYYMMDD-ClientName-#### (e.g., 20240315-MarinaBay-0001.DNG). Original camera filenames are meaningless and collide across shoots.
5. **Apply Develop Preset at Import:** Create a baseline import preset (slight highlight recovery, shadow lift, standard lens correction) to save time on every image
6. **Build Smart Previews:** Check this option to allow editing even when the source drive is disconnected

**Post-import rating workflow:**
* Review at 1:1 (100% zoom) for focus and technical quality; mark rejects with X
* Flag selects with P (Pick flag) for images worth developing
* Use star ratings to tier selects: 5-star = hero images, 3-star = supporting selects, 1-star = backups
* Filter to Picks only before beginning the edit pass

### Organizing Across Projects

**Folders panel:** Mirrors your actual file system. Organize by Year -> Client -> Shoot date.

**Collections:** Virtual groupings independent of file location. Maintain a Best Aerial Portfolio collection with hero images from all projects, and a Current Work collection for active client projects. Collections are views, not copies -- no additional storage used.

sub_units.title: Exposure, Tone & White Balance Correction
sub_units.description: Mastering the Lightroom Develop module: the tonal hierarchy (Exposure, Highlights, Shadows, Whites, Blacks), reading the histogram, white balance adjustment, and the Tone Curve for precise control over aerial stills.
sub_units.text_content:
### The Lightroom Histogram

The histogram shows tonal distribution from pure black (left) to pure white (right). It is your primary exposure reference -- more reliable than the screen on any un-calibrated monitor.

* **Clipping indicators:** The triangles in the top corners of the histogram. Left = shadow clipping. Right = highlight clipping. Click either triangle to show a colored overlay on the image identifying clipped pixels.
* **Goal for aerial stills:** No highlight clipping in important areas (sky, clouds, bright facades). Slight shadow clipping in the deepest shadows is acceptable for most work.

### The Basic Panel: Tonal Hierarchy

Adjust these controls in order for most efficient results:

**1. Exposure:** Overall brightness. Start here. For scenes with mixed bright sky and dark ground, set for the midtones and correct extremes separately.

**2. Highlights:** Controls the upper tonal quarter. Drag left (-) to recover blown sky and reflective surfaces. RAW highlights can be recovered by 2-3 stops. This is the most-used adjustment in aerial photography.

**3. Shadows:** Controls the lower tonal quarter. Drag right (+) to open dark ground areas, shaded vegetation, and building shadows. Excessive lifting adds noise; manage with noise reduction in the Detail panel.

**4. Whites:** Sets the maximum white point. Trim to the edge of the histogram without clipping.

**5. Blacks:** Sets the minimum black point. Pulling left adds richness and prevents the image from looking milky or flat.

**Typical aerial real-estate edit pattern:**
* Highlights: -60 to -80 (recover sky detail)
* Shadows: +30 to +60 (open shaded ground)
* Whites: -5 to -15 (prevent specular clipping)
* Blacks: -15 to -25 (add tonal richness)

This W-shaped edit recovers both extremes toward the center, maximizing visible detail throughout the scene. It is the single most effective standard edit for aerial real-estate and landscape photography.

### White Balance in Lightroom

White balance is fully adjustable in RAW files with no quality penalty:

**Temperature slider:** Left (cooler/blue) or right (warmer/orange). For real estate and landscape work, slightly warm is conventionally more appealing (5700-6200K even when shot at 5600K).

**Tint slider:** Corrects green-magenta casts, often introduced by ND filters. If the image has a slight green cast, move toward +Magenta.

**White Balance Selector (eyedropper):** Click on a neutral gray area in the image (concrete, overcast sky, white wall in open shade). Lightroom sets Temperature and Tint to neutralize that area.

**Batch WB correction:** Set white balance on the best representative image from a location sequence, then Sync the correction to all similar images from that shoot segment.

### Tone Curve

The Tone Curve provides finer tonal control than the Basic panel sliders:

**Point Curve:** A fully manual Bezier curve from black (bottom-left) to white (top-right). Click to add control points; drag up to brighten, down to darken that tonal region.

**The S-curve for aerial stills:** A gentle S-curve (raise the Lights region, lower the Darks region) adds contrast and visual punch while preserving detail at the extremes. This is the most universally useful Tone Curve adjustment for outdoor and aerial photography.

**RGB Curves:** Separate curves for Red, Green, and Blue channels allow color-specific tonal adjustments -- useful for warming shadows by adding red in the lower curve, or deepening sky blue by reducing blue in the highlights.

### Batch Sync Workflow

For a shoot with dozens of images from the same location and light:
1. Edit the best representative image fully
2. Select it + all similar images in the Filmstrip (Shift-click to range-select)
3. Click Sync Settings -- choose which settings to copy (White Balance, Tone, Curve, Lens Corrections)
4. Click Synchronize
5. Review and individually adjust images where exposure differs

**Auto Sync:** Toggle in the toolbar to instantly apply every slider move to all selected images simultaneously -- useful for large batch adjustments.

sub_units.title: Color Grading & AI Masking for Aerial Stills
sub_units.description: Using Lightroom's HSL/Color panel, Color Grading wheels, and AI-powered Masking (Select Sky, Select Subject, Gradient, Brush) to craft polished aerial images with targeted, independent adjustments.
sub_units.text_content:
### HSL / Color Panel

The HSL (Hue-Saturation-Luminance) panel gives independent control over eight color ranges: Red, Orange, Yellow, Green, Aqua, Blue, Purple, Magenta. It is the most powerful color tool for aerial photography because every landscape or architectural element can be tuned without affecting adjacent colors.

**Three sub-panels:**
* **Hue:** Shifts a color range toward an adjacent color. Example: Push Yellow toward Orange for warmer, golden vegetation in autumn light.
* **Saturation:** Increases or decreases vividness of a specific color range. Example: Boost Blue for a deeper sky; reduce Orange to tone down harsh brick facades.
* **Luminance:** Brightens or darkens a specific color range without shifting its hue. Example: Darken Blue luminance to make the sky richer and more dramatic.

**Common aerial HSL adjustments:**

| Color | Typical Adjustment | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Blue / Aqua | Saturation +20, Luminance -15 | Deeper, richer sky |
| Green | Hue -10 (toward yellow), Saturation +10 | More natural, vibrant vegetation |
| Orange | Saturation -10 | Reduce harsh building facade intensity |
| Yellow | Luminance +10 | Brighten dry grass, sand, golden fields |
| Aqua | Saturation +15 | Enhance water clarity and color depth |

**Targeted Adjustment Tool (TAT):** The circle icon in the HSL panel header. Click and drag directly on any color in the image -- dragging up increases, dragging down decreases the active HSL property for that specific color. Much faster and more accurate than moving sliders.

### Color Grading Panel

Separate color wheels for Shadows, Midtones, Highlights, and a Global wheel -- equivalent to DaVinci Resolve's color wheels applied to stills.

**For aerial landscape photography:**
* Shadows: Push slightly toward blue/teal -- adds atmospheric depth and separates ground from sky
* Highlights: Push slightly toward warm orange -- enhances golden-hour tones; makes bright surfaces feel warmer
* This is the orange-teal grade applied to still photos: subtle, cinematic, and effective for portfolio work

**For real-estate deliverables:** Keep Color Grading minimal and neutral. Clients need accurate color representation of their property, not a cinematic interpretation.

### AI Masking for Targeted Adjustments

Lightroom's AI Masking (2022+) is the most significant advance in photo editing for aerial work in years:

**Select Sky:**
In the Masking panel (Shift+W), click 'Select Sky.' Lightroom's AI automatically selects the sky with high accuracy -- around complex tree canopies, building silhouettes, and antenna arrays. Once selected:
* Independently reduce highlights and boost blue saturation in the sky
* Apply a different white balance to the sky vs. the ground
* Darken sky luminance for drama without affecting the foreground

**Select Subject:**
Selects the main subject (building, landmark, vehicle). For real-estate work: make the property brighter and more saturated than the surrounding landscape to draw the viewer's eye.

**Linear Gradient:**
A linear mask from top to bottom (or any angle). The post-production equivalent of a graduated ND filter. Use to darken the sky half of an image, or apply a graduated white balance shift to balance sky and ground exposure.

**Radial Gradient:**
An elliptical mask for vignettes, soft spotlight effects, or centering attention on a specific area. Invert to create a center-brightening vignette that draws the eye to the subject.

**Brush Mask:**
Manually paint a mask for complex shapes AI cannot handle, or for precise corrections to specific small areas. Useful for adding exposure to a single shaded building while leaving adjacent structures untouched.

**Combining masks:** Use 'Add' and 'Subtract' within a mask to refine AI selections. For example: Select Sky, then Subtract the AI's incorrect inclusion of a white cloud-like building facade.

### Removing Distracting Elements

**Heal Brush (Content-Aware Fill):**
* Remove parked cars from real-estate driveways
* Remove power lines crossing a landscape
* Remove the drone's own shadow visible in straight-down nadir shots
* Remove litter, signage, or construction debris from otherwise clean property shots

**Heal vs. Clone:**
* Heal: Blends the sample with surrounding texture -- better for organic areas (grass, water, foliage)
* Clone: Copies exactly from the source sample -- better for geometric patterns (roads, tile, concrete)

**AI Content Fill (Lightroom 2024+):** Click in the Healing tool, check 'Content Aware,' and drag over the element to remove. Lightroom's AI analyzes the surrounding content and synthesizes a replacement. Works well for isolated elements over flat backgrounds. May require manual source selection for complex backgrounds.

### Creating Presets for Consistent Style

1. Develop a representative image to your desired look
2. Presets panel (left sidebar) -> '+' -> Create Preset
3. Name descriptively: Aerial-RealEstate-Standard, Aerial-Landscape-GoldenHour, Aerial-Social-Dramatic
4. Include: Color, Tone Curve, HSL, Color Grading, Detail (sharpening/noise reduction)
5. Apply to any image in one click, or apply at Import for immediate baseline consistency

Maintain separate presets for different content types -- a dramatic landscape preset applied to client real-estate deliverables will distort colors and damage the professional relationship.

sub_units.title: HDR Merge, Panorama Stitching & Sky Replacement
sub_units.description: Advanced aerial photography post-production: merging AEB bracket sets for extended dynamic range, stitching multi-frame panoramas, and AI sky replacement for real-estate and commercial photography.
sub_units.text_content:
### HDR Merge in Lightroom

When a single RAW's dynamic range is insufficient for a scene (e.g., a property shot with bright windows and dark interiors, or a landscape with a blown sky), HDR bracketing and merging provides the solution.

**On-site AEB capture for drones:**
* Set the drone camera to Auto Exposure Bracketing (AEB): typically 3 or 5 frames at +/-2 EV steps
* The drone must hover completely stationary during AEB capture -- any positional drift causes ghosting in the merged image
* Calm conditions (low wind) are required for reliable AEB results
* DJI cameras: AEB available in the camera settings menu; captures 3, 5, or 7 frames automatically in sequence

**Merging in Lightroom:**
1. Select all bracketed frames in the Library or Develop module
2. Photo -> Photo Merge -> HDR (Ctrl/Cmd+H)
3. Lightroom aligns frames (corrects position drift) and generates a merged 16-bit .DNG
4. Options:
   * Auto Align: Always check
   * Auto Settings: Optional starting point for tonal adjustments
   * Deghost Amount: Set to Low for most scenes; use Medium/High only for large moving elements (water, foliage)
5. The merged HDR DNG appears in your catalog alongside source files -- edit it like any other RAW with dramatically expanded tonal range

**When to use HDR for aerial:**
* Nadir shots of properties with extreme contrast (black rooftops vs. white concrete)
* Sunrise/sunset scenes with bright sky requiring separate sky and ground exposure
* Any scene where a single RAW's highlight recovery is insufficient even at maximum negative Highlights

### Aerial Panoramas

Aerial panoramas extend the field of view beyond a single frame -- wide 180 or 360 degree vistas, ultra-high-resolution cityscapes, and interactive VR spheres for real-estate virtual tours.

**Capture requirements:**
* Each frame must overlap adjacent frames by 40-50% horizontally and 30-40% vertically
* The drone must hold a fixed altitude and position for each frame; capture in calm conditions
* Expose consistently across all frames (use manual exposure, not auto) -- exposure changes between frames create visible seams

**Capture patterns:**
* Horizontal strip: Drone holds position and rotates through yaw in 10-15 degree increments. Captures a wide horizontal panorama. Best for landscapes and cityscapes.
* Grid: Multiple rows with horizontal and vertical coverage. For ultra-high-resolution and 360 sphere panoramas.
* DJI Sphere mode: Automated in-app capture of a full sphere panorama. DJI's app stitches the sphere. Suitable for Google Street View style virtual tours.

**Stitching in Lightroom:**
1. Select all panorama source images
2. Photo -> Photo Merge -> Panorama (Ctrl/Cmd+M)
3. Projection options:
   * Spherical: Best for wide-angle and 360 panoramas; handles optical distortion
   * Cylindrical: Best for long horizontal strips; keeps vertical lines straight
   * Perspective: Best for narrow panoramas (3-4 images); distorts at the edges for wide panoramas
4. Auto Crop: Trims irregular edges. Disable and use Fill Edges (AI content fill) to maximize resolution without cropping.
5. Stitched panorama saved as a new .DNG file -- fully editable RAW

**When Lightroom fails:** For panoramas with significant parallax errors (close subjects at different distances), use dedicated stitching software: PTGui (professional, highly accurate) or Microsoft ICE (free, good quality).

### AI Sky Replacement

Sky replacement substitutes a flat or uninteresting sky with a more dramatic one. It is standard practice in commercial real-estate photography when shoot windows are limited and weather is uncooperative.

**Lightroom masking approach:**
1. Masking panel -> Select Sky (AI selection)
2. Adjust selected sky independently: boost blue saturation, reduce highlights, add drama -- without changing the ground
3. For a full sky replacement (actual different sky image), use Adobe Photoshop

**Photoshop Sky Replacement:**
1. Open image in Photoshop (Edit in Photoshop from Lightroom, Ctrl/Cmd+E)
2. Edit -> Sky Replacement
3. Browse the built-in sky library or load your own sky photographs
4. Adobe Sensei AI detects and masks the sky edge -- around trees, antennas, and building silhouettes
5. Adjust the replacement sky: scale, shift, and match brightness to the ground lighting
6. Edge refinement tools for complex foreground/sky intersections

**Sky replacement ethics and disclosure:**
* Commercial real estate and marketing photography: Industry-standard practice; disclose if the client asks directly
* Journalistic, documentary, and editorial photography: Not acceptable -- discloses compositional changes
* Photography competitions: Check specific rules; many prohibit sky replacement
* When uncertain about the client's expectations, ask before applying

sub_units.title: Exporting Aerial Stills for Every Deliverable
sub_units.description: Complete export settings for MLS real-estate listings, client full-resolution archive delivery, social media platforms, and large-format print, plus watermarking, sharpening, and AI noise reduction workflows.
sub_units.text_content:
### Lightroom Export Workflow

File -> Export (Ctrl/Cmd+Shift+E). Build a preset library for each delivery destination -- this reduces every export to a few clicks.

### Export Settings by Destination

**MLS / Real Estate Web Listings:**
* File Format: JPEG
* Color Space: sRGB (required for web and MLS systems)
* Bit Depth: 8 bit
* Image Sizing: Resize to Long Edge 2000-3000 px (check your MLS system's requirements)
* Resolution: 72 ppi (irrelevant for digital display -- only matters for print)
* Output Sharpening: Screen, Standard
* Metadata: Strip all metadata (removes GPS coordinates for privacy)
* File Name: Apply your naming preset

**Client Full-Resolution Archive:**
* File Format: JPEG Quality 95-100 (or TIFF for lossless delivery)
* Color Space: sRGB for JPEG; AdobeRGB for TIFF if the client uses a color-managed workflow
* Image Sizing: Unchecked (full original resolution)
* Metadata: Copyright and contact info only
* Deliver via: Dropbox, Google Drive, WeTransfer, or Frame.io

**Social Media:**

| Platform | Resolution | Aspect Ratio | Format |
|---|---|---|---|
| Instagram Feed (landscape) | 1080 x 608 px | 16:9 | JPEG |
| Instagram Feed (square) | 1080 x 1080 px | 1:1 | JPEG |
| Instagram Portrait / Reels | 1080 x 1350 px | 4:5 | JPEG |
| LinkedIn | 1200 x 628 px | 1.91:1 | JPEG |
| X / Twitter | 1200 x 675 px | 16:9 | JPEG |

For all social platforms: Color Space sRGB, Quality 85-90. Platforms re-compress regardless; higher quality minimizes double-compression artifacts.

**Large-Format Print:**
* File Format: TIFF (lossless) or JPEG Quality 100
* Color Space: AdobeRGB or ProPhoto (wider gamuts for print; only use if the print provider has a color-managed workflow)
* Image Sizing: Do NOT downsize -- preserve full resolution
* Resolution: 300 ppi for standard print viewing; 150-200 ppi for large-format display viewed from 5+ feet
* Most modern drone cameras (~12-20 MP) produce files suitable for prints up to approximately 16x24 inches at 300 ppi. For larger prints, upscale using Lightroom's Enhance -> Super Resolution (AI upscaling to 4x resolution) or Adobe Camera Raw's Super Resolution.

### Output Sharpening

Lightroom applies a second sharpening pass at export optimized for the output medium:
* Screen: For web and digital delivery
* Glossy Paper / Matte Paper: For print, with strength calibrated to the paper surface absorption

### Watermarking

In the Export dialog -> Watermarking section:
* Text watermark: Name, website, or copyright notice. Set font, opacity, and position (lower-right is standard).
* Graphic watermark: PNG logo with transparency.

**When to watermark:**
* Proof images sent to clients for review: watermark with PROOF or your logo
* Portfolio images posted online: subtle copyright text
* Final client deliverables: No watermark -- client has paid for unlicensed files

### Noise Reduction in Lightroom

**Detail Panel -- Traditional Noise Reduction:**
* Luminance: Reduces grain in tonal values. Use 25-50 for ISO 400-1600 aerial work. Above 60, fine detail begins to soften.
* Color: Reduces colored speckles in shadow areas. Default of 25 handles most cases.
* Masking (Detail Panel -- Sharpening): Hold Alt/Option while dragging the Masking slider to see a white/black overlay. Restrict sharpening to edges and detail areas (masking value 40-60) to prevent noise amplification in smooth sky areas.

**AI Denoise (Lightroom 2023+):**
Photo -> Enhance -> Denoise. Lightroom's AI generates a new DNG file with dramatically superior noise reduction compared to the traditional sliders -- fine detail is preserved at ISOs where traditional NR turns everything to watercolor. Processing takes 10-60 seconds per image. Essential for blue-hour, low-light, and indoor aerial work. The resulting Denoise DNG is a full-quality RAW file editable with all Lightroom tools.

**Super Resolution (Enhance -> Super Resolution):**
AI upscaling that quadruples pixel count (doubles each dimension) using machine learning. Use before final export for large-format print, or for images that need to be cropped significantly while maintaining deliverable resolution. Takes 10-30 seconds per image.

units.title: Introduction to Video Editing & Software
units.sub_title: Building Your Edit Suite and Mastering the Workflow
units.description: An introduction to professional video editing software options, how to set up an efficient edit workflow, import and organize drone footage, understand the timeline and interface, and apply basic cutting and trimming techniques to build the first assembly cut.
units.text_content: Video editing is where the creative and technical work of aerial production comes to life. The edit suite is where individual shots become sequences, sequences become stories, and raw footage becomes something an audience can feel. This is the largest section of the course because editing is where most of the actual production time is spent — a typical professional will spend 3-5 hours in the edit for every 1 hour of drone flying. Mastery of the edit workflow is what separates occasional drone hobbyists from working professionals.

sub_units.title: Overview of Video Editing Software
sub_units.description: Comparison of the major professional editing platforms — DaVinci Resolve, Premiere Pro, Final Cut Pro, and CapCut — with recommendations for specific workflows and career paths.
sub_units.text_content:
Choosing an editing application is a foundational decision that affects your workflow, your compatibility with collaborators, and your career options. The major platforms are DaVinci Resolve, Adobe Premiere Pro, Apple Final Cut Pro, and CapCut (for social media).

### DaVinci Resolve (Blackmagic Design)

**Best for:** Color grading, cinema work, LOG footage, professional post-production pipelines

**Pricing:** Free version is extremely capable. DaVinci Resolve Studio (~$295 one-time) adds noise reduction, collaborative tools, and some advanced effects.

**Strengths:**
* The industry standard for professional color grading — the Color page is unmatched by any competitor
* Full-featured editing timeline (Cut page for speed, Edit page for precision)
* Fusion visual effects compositor built in
* Fairlight audio post-production suite built in
* Best performance with LOG and RAW footage from drone cameras
* No subscription — one-time purchase or free forever

**Weaknesses:**
* Steeper learning curve for beginners
* Requires a reasonably powerful computer for real-time 4K playback
* Less widely used in certain broadcast/news production environments

**Recommended for:** Anyone serious about color grading LOG drone footage, cinema and commercial production, long-form documentary work.

### Adobe Premiere Pro

**Best for:** Broadcast, news, YouTube, social media, collaborative productions

**Pricing:** Subscription via Adobe Creative Cloud (~$55/month individual, or Creative Cloud All Apps)

**Strengths:**
* Extremely deep integration with Adobe ecosystem (After Effects for motion graphics, Photoshop for images, Audition for audio)
* Extensive plugin ecosystem (transitions, effects, presets)
* Industry standard in broadcast, advertising agencies, and YouTube production
* Good multi-cam editing capabilities
* Strong media management with Adobe Bridge / Productions

**Weaknesses:**
* Subscription cost is a recurring expense
* Color grading via Lumetri Color is capable but not as powerful as DaVinci Resolve
* Can be resource-intensive

**Recommended for:** Operators working within Adobe Creative Cloud teams, broadcast clients, YouTube creators who need motion graphics integration.

### Apple Final Cut Pro

**Best for:** Mac-only workflow, fast turnaround social media and YouTube content, event videography

**Pricing:** ~$300 one-time purchase (Mac App Store)

**Strengths:**
* Extremely fast performance on Apple Silicon Macs (M1, M2, M3) — native ProRes support handles 4K/6K/8K footage effortlessly
* Magnetic Timeline is intuitive and fast for rough assembly
* Excellent for organizing large volumes of event footage quickly
* Good multicam editing

**Weaknesses:**
* Mac only — no Windows version
* Less powerful color grading than DaVinci Resolve
* Less plugin ecosystem than Premiere Pro
* Not the industry standard outside of high-end post houses and YouTube

**Recommended for:** Mac users doing event coverage, real estate, and social media content where speed of turnaround is the priority.

### CapCut

**Best for:** Short-form social media content (Instagram Reels, TikTok, YouTube Shorts)

**Pricing:** Free with paid Pro tier

**Strengths:**
* Extremely fast for short-form content with AI auto-cut features
* Built-in templates, trending audio, auto-captions
* Mobile app allows editing anywhere

**Weaknesses:**
* Not suitable for professional deliverables beyond social media
* Limited color grading tools
* Not a professional workflow tool

**Recommended for:** Social media-focused content creators who need fast social clips. Not a substitute for DaVinci Resolve or Premiere Pro for professional work.

### Hardware Requirements

**Minimum for 4K drone footage editing:**
* CPU: Modern 8-core or better (Intel Core i7/i9, AMD Ryzen 7/9, Apple M1+)
* RAM: 32GB minimum, 64GB recommended for 4K/LOG workflows
* GPU: Dedicated GPU with 8GB+ VRAM for GPU-accelerated decoding (NVIDIA RTX 3060+, AMD RX 6700+)
* Storage: NVMe SSD for project drive (minimum 2TB), separate backup drive
* Monitor: A properly calibrated monitor is essential for color work — a cheap un-calibrated monitor renders all color grading work meaningless

**Monitor calibration:** Use a hardware colorimeter (X-Rite i1Display Pro, Calibrite ColorChecker Display) to calibrate your monitor to standard color temperature (6500K D65) and gamma (2.2 or sRGB). Without calibration, what looks correct on your screen may look dramatically different on a client's or viewer's screen.

sub_units.title: Importing, Organizing & Managing Drone Footage
sub_units.description: A professional import and media management workflow in DaVinci Resolve and Premiere Pro — bins, metadata, smart folders, and proxy generation for smooth real-time editing.
sub_units.text_content:
How you organize footage at import time determines how smoothly everything goes afterward. A well-organized project opens quickly, allows instant access to any clip, and makes it easy to hand off to a collaborator. A disorganized project costs hours of time searching for clips and can lead to disasters like missing media or accidental overwrites.

### Pre-Import File Structure

Before importing anything, establish a consistent folder structure on your drives:

```
Project_ClientName_YYYYMMDD/
├── 01_RAW_FOOTAGE/
│   ├── Card01/
│   ├── Card02/
├── 02_PROJECT_FILES/
│   ├── Resolve/ or Premiere/
├── 03_AUDIO/
│   ├── Music/
│   ├── SFX/
├── 04_GRAPHICS/
├── 05_EXPORTS/
│   ├── Client_Delivery/
│   ├── Social_Media/
├── 06_BACKUPS/
```

Never edit directly from memory cards or external drives that are not reliably connected. Copy all footage to your project drive before importing into your editing software.

### Importing in DaVinci Resolve

**Media Pool organization:**
1. Create a new project with the correct timeline frame rate (24fps, 30fps, etc.) — this cannot be changed easily after starting
2. In the Media Pool, create bins: 'Aerial_Raw,' 'Ground_Raw,' 'Audio_Music,' 'Audio_SFX,' 'Graphics'
3. Use the Media page to browse to your footage folders and drag clips into the appropriate bins
4. Do NOT move or rename files after importing — Resolve stores absolute paths, and moved files become 'missing media'

**Proxy workflow for 4K LOG footage:**
High-resolution LOG footage can be demanding to play back smoothly in real-time. Generate proxies (low-resolution versions) for editing and switch back to full-resolution for export:
1. In the Media Pool, select all clips → right-click → 'Generate Optimized Media' (ProRes Proxy on Mac, DNxHR LB on Windows)
2. Resolve automatically uses the proxy for playback and the original for export
3. Check 'Use optimized media if available' in Playback settings

### Importing in Premiere Pro

**Project Panel structure:**
1. Create bins: 'Sequences', 'Aerial', 'Ground Level', 'Audio', 'Graphics'
2. File → Import → navigate to footage folders
3. Or use the Media Browser to browse footage while previewing thumbnails

**Proxy workflow:**
1. In the Media Browser, select clips → right-click → 'Proxy → Create Proxies'
2. Choose a proxy preset (ProRes Proxy or H.264 Low Res)
3. Toggle proxy viewing with the proxy button in the Program Monitor

### Reviewing and Rating Footage (Selects)

Before editing, review all footage and mark your selects — the clips worth using:

**In DaVinci Resolve:**
* Use the Color Flags (Green = select, Red = reject) or Star ratings
* In the Color page, mark IN/OUT points for the usable portion of each clip

**In Premiere Pro:**
* Mark clips as 'Good' using Cmd/Ctrl+G in the Media Browser
* Use markers to mark the best portions of each clip

**Efficiency tip:** Review footage at 2x or 4x speed for long, similar shots (simple flyovers, holds). Drop to 1x for shots requiring precise assessment of stability and focus. You are looking for: smooth motion (no bumps, no vibration jello), correct focus and exposure, usable framing, and absence of unexpected intrusions (birds, aircraft, vehicles).

sub_units.title: Understanding the Timeline & Interface
sub_units.description: A thorough orientation to the editing timeline in DaVinci Resolve and Premiere Pro — tracks, playhead, timecode, in/out points, and the key keyboard shortcuts that make editing fast.
sub_units.text_content:
The editing timeline is a visual representation of your edit in time. Clips are placed on tracks (video tracks above the center, audio tracks below), and the playhead marks the current position in the sequence. Mastering the timeline interface and keyboard shortcuts is one of the most important investments you can make in editing speed.

### Timeline Fundamentals

**Timecode:** Video time is measured in Hours:Minutes:Seconds:Frames (HH:MM:SS:FF). At 24fps, one second = 24 frames; frame 25 is the start of the next second. Understanding timecode allows precise navigation and clip placement.

**Tracks:** 
* Video tracks (V1, V2, V3...) stack upward from the center. V1 is the primary video track. V2 above it will appear on top of V1 in the frame (useful for titles, overlays, and picture-in-picture).
* Audio tracks (A1, A2, A3...) stack downward. A1 is typically music; A2 is dialogue/narration; A3-A4 are sound effects.

**Track targeting:** When you press keyboard shortcuts like 'V' (cut), the operation affects only the currently targeted tracks. Click the track header to target/de-target tracks before operating.

### Essential Keyboard Shortcuts (DaVinci Resolve)

| Action | Shortcut |
|---|---|
| Play / Pause | Space |
| Play Forward at Speed | J K L (L = fast forward, J = rewind, K = pause) |
| Mark In Point | I |
| Mark Out Point | O |
| Insert clip at playhead | F9 |
| Overwrite clip at playhead | F10 |
| Blade (cut) clip | Cmd+B / Ctrl+B |
| Delete clip (leave gap) | Delete |
| Ripple delete (close gap) | Fn+Delete / Shift+Delete |
| Undo | Cmd+Z / Ctrl+Z |
| Toggle playback resolution | None (set in Playback menu) |
| Go to start | Home |
| Go to end | End |
| Step forward one frame | Right Arrow |
| Step backward one frame | Left Arrow |

### Essential Keyboard Shortcuts (Premiere Pro)

| Action | Shortcut |
|---|---|
| Play / Pause | Space |
| JKL playback | J K L |
| Mark In | I |
| Mark Out | O |
| Insert | , (comma) |
| Overwrite | . (period) |
| Razor Tool | C |
| Selection Tool | V |
| Ripple Delete | Shift+Delete |
| Undo | Cmd+Z / Ctrl+Z |
| Match Frame | F |
| Render In/Out | Enter |

### The Three-Point Edit System

The foundation of professional editing is the three-point edit:
1. Mark an IN point on the source clip (where the usable portion starts)
2. Mark an OUT point on the source clip (where it ends)
3. Mark an IN point on the timeline (where the clip should begin)
4. Insert or Overwrite the clip — the software automatically calculates the end point on the timeline

This allows precise placement of exactly the right portion of a clip at exactly the right position in the sequence without manual dragging.

### Timeline Navigation for Long Projects

* **Zoom in/out:** Cmd+= / Cmd+- in Resolve; + and - in Premiere
* **Scroll to playhead:** P in Resolve (auto-scroll)
* **Jump to specific timecode:** Type the timecode on a numeric keypad; the playhead jumps to that position
* Use colored markers to mark important sections of the timeline: the intro, each main segment, the outro. This allows instant navigation in long projects.

sub_units.title: Basic Cutting & Trimming Techniques
sub_units.description: The practical mechanics of building an edit: placing clips, trimming handles, performing ripple and roll edits, and building a rough cut from drone footage.
sub_units.text_content:
Editing is the art of making decisions about time — what to keep, what to remove, and exactly where to cut. The technical mechanics of cutting and trimming are learned skills that become automatic with practice. This unit covers the fundamental operations that form the backbone of every edit.

### Types of Edits

**Hard cut:** An instantaneous transition from one clip to the next with no visual effect. The default and most common edit type. When executed at the right moment (on a beat, on a movement, on a blink), a hard cut is invisible.

**Dissolve / Cross-Dissolve:** A gradual transition where the outgoing clip fades out while the incoming clip fades in. Used to indicate a passage of time, a gentle transition between scenes, or a dreamlike quality. Overuse makes an edit feel soft and indecisive.

**Fade to black / Fade from black:** Used to begin and end sequences, mark major section breaks, or indicate a significant passage of time. Fade duration (fast = dramatic; slow = contemplative) affects emotional impact.

### Trimming Techniques

**Single-side trim (Trim In / Trim Out):** Moving only one edge of a clip. Extends or shortens the clip from that edge, shifting all subsequent clips downstream (ripple) or leaving a gap.

**Ripple Edit:** Moving a clip's edge while automatically closing or opening the gap on the timeline. This preserves the timing of all subsequent clips relative to the edit. Used when shortening a clip you want subsequent clips to immediately follow.

**Roll Edit:** Moving the edit point between two adjacent clips simultaneously — as you extend the outgoing clip, you shorten the incoming clip by the same amount. Total timeline duration stays the same. Used when you need to adjust exactly where the cut occurs between two clips.

**Slip Edit:** Moves the content of a clip within its duration without changing where it sits on the timeline or how long it is. Used to choose a different portion of the source clip without disturbing the surrounding edit.

**Slide Edit:** Moves the position of a clip on the timeline, while automatically adjusting adjacent clips to fill the gap. The moved clip's content stays the same.

### Building the Rough Cut from Drone Footage

**Assembly cut:** Place all your selected clips on the timeline in roughly the intended order without worrying about precise cut points. Goal: get everything on the timeline so you can see the material's potential.

**Structure first:** Identify the strongest shots — the shots that will anchor the edit — and place those first. Build the rest of the edit around them, not the other way around. A compelling establishing shot, a dramatic reveal, and a satisfying closing shot form the backbone.

**Rough timing:** Trim each clip to approximately the right length. Rule of thumb: most aerial drone shots work well at 3-8 seconds in a fast-paced edit, 8-20 seconds in a contemplative edit. Avoid the common mistake of letting shots run too long — audiences read aerial shots quickly.

**Cut to the music:** If music is available early, rough-cut the edit to the music's tempo and energy arc. Mark the significant beats (kick drum, musical phrase changes) with markers on the timeline and align cuts to those beats. Music and picture that breathe together feel professional; picture that ignores the music feels amateurish.

**The three-cut rule:** Every clip in the timeline should be there for a reason. If you cannot state why a clip is there, it should be cut. If the edit flows naturally without a shot, remove it — the best edits feel both complete and lean.

units.title: Color Grading Aerial Footage
units.description: A comprehensive color grading workflow using DaVinci Resolve: color correction vs. creative grading, working with LOG footage and LUTs, primary color correction using wheels and curves, secondary adjustments with qualifiers and masks, and building a repeatable cinematic look.
units.text_content: Color grading is the process of establishing the visual mood and emotional tone of a piece through the deliberate manipulation of color, contrast, and saturation. For aerial footage — often shot in LOG with a wide dynamic range — color grading is where the flat, technical capture is transformed into the rich, emotional image the viewer experiences. A well-graded piece of aerial footage communicates more than just a location — it communicates how to feel about it.

sub_units.title: Color Correction vs. Color Grading
sub_units.description: Understanding the two-stage color pipeline: technical correction to establish a clean, accurate baseline, followed by creative grading to establish the visual look.
sub_units.text_content:
Color work in post-production has two distinct phases that are often confused but serve different purposes:

### Color Correction (Technical Stage)

Color correction is the process of making the image technically accurate — matching the scene as it appeared in reality, removing color casts from imperfect white balance, balancing exposure inconsistencies between clips, and ensuring that flesh tones (and neutral tones like gray and white) appear correctly.

**Goals of color correction:**
* Set black point (shadows should be at 0% luminance unless artistic intent dictates otherwise)
* Set white point (highlights should be at 100% luminance, with detail preserved)
* Correct white balance errors (remove green cast from certain ND filters, correct AWB fluctuations)
* Match clips that were shot at different times or in different light conditions so they look consistent in a sequence
* Ensure LOG footage is converted to the correct output color space (Rec.709 for SDR, P3 or Rec.2020 for HDR)

**Tools used for correction:** Scopes (Waveform, Parade, Vectorscope, Histogram), Color Wheels (Lift/Gamma/Gain), Curves, Log Wheels

### Color Grading (Creative Stage)

Color grading is the application of a deliberate creative look after the technical baseline is established. The grade serves the story — it communicates mood, period, geography, and emotion through color.

**Examples of creative grade intentions:**
* Warm, golden look → summer, nostalgia, intimacy
* Cool, desaturated look → clinical, isolated, futuristic, cold environment
* High contrast, crushed blacks → dramatic, cinematic, noir
* Low contrast, lifted blacks → vintage, faded, '70s film look
* Complementary color split (orange shadows / teal highlights) → cinematic Hollywood look, warmth-cool contrast

**Tools used for grading:** The same technical tools, but applied for creative rather than corrective purposes, plus: Curves (hue vs. saturation, hue vs. hue, luminance vs. saturation), Qualifiers, Power Windows (masks)

### The Two-Stage Workflow in DaVinci Resolve

DaVinci Resolve's node structure makes the two-stage workflow explicit:

**Recommended node structure for a grade:**
1. **Node 1 — Technical Correction:** Apply LUT to convert LOG → Rec.709; adjust lift/gamma/gain to correct exposure
2. **Node 2 — Color Balance:** Correct white balance; neutralize color casts; match clips
3. **Node 3 — Creative Grade:** Apply the look — push shadows cool, pull highlights warm, adjust contrast, desaturate or pump saturation
4. **Node 4 — Selective Adjustments:** Sky mask for independent sky grading; skin tone protection; isolated adjustments

Keeping each stage in a separate node allows you to adjust any stage independently without affecting the others.

sub_units.title: Working with LOG Footage & LUTs in DaVinci Resolve
sub_units.description: Setting up a DaVinci Resolve project for LOG footage, applying technical LUTs for camera transforms, and building a node structure that separates technical conversion from creative grading.
sub_units.text_content:
DaVinci Resolve is the industry's leading tool for working with LOG footage. Its node-based color pipeline and Color Science framework make it possible to work with any camera's LOG format and deliver for any display standard.

### Project Setup for LOG Footage

**Timeline color space:**
Before grading, set the project's color science and timeline color space:
1. Project Settings → Color Management
2. Set Color Science to 'DaVinci YRGB Color Managed' or 'DaVinci YRGB'
3. If color managed: set Input Color Space to the camera's LOG format (e.g., DJI D-Log M, Rec.709), and Output Color Space to Rec.709 Gamma 2.4 for standard monitors
4. DaVinci will automatically apply a camera transform when you place the clip — allowing you to see the converted image before any manual adjustments

**Alternatively — Manual LUT method:**
1. Set Color Science to 'DaVinci YRGB'
2. Apply the camera manufacturer's technical LUT as the first node on every clip
3. DJI provides free D-Log → Rec.709 LUTs on their website for all camera models

### How to Apply a LUT in DaVinci Resolve

**Applying a LUT to a node:**
1. In the Color page, right-click any node → 'Add LUT'
2. Navigate to your LUT file (.cube format)
3. The LUT is applied and the clip appears in the target color space

**3D LUT vs. 1D LUT:**
* **1D LUT:** Adjusts luminance (brightness curve) independently. Simple, limited.
* **3D LUT:** Adjusts color, luminance, and saturation simultaneously. Far more powerful — the standard for camera transforms and creative looks.

**Gallery Stills for matching:**
After grading the first clip, save a Gallery Still (Cmd+G) — a snapshot of the grade. You can then apply that grade's nodes to other clips by dragging the still to them, or use Remote Grades to apply it across all clips automatically.

### Color Wheels: Lift, Gamma, Gain

The primary color wheels in DaVinci Resolve control three tonal regions:

* **Lift (Shadows):** Adjusts the darkest tones in the image. Dragging down darkens shadows; dragging up lifts them. Pushing the shadow color wheel toward blue creates cool shadows; toward orange creates warm shadows.
* **Gamma (Midtones):** Adjusts the middle tones without affecting the extremes. Most critical for skin tone and midrange landscape tones.
* **Gain (Highlights):** Adjusts the brightest tones. Dragging the Gain wheel toward a color tints the highlights.

**Offset:** A master control that shifts all tonal regions simultaneously. Used to correct overall exposure rather than targeting specific ranges.

### Log Wheels vs. Color Wheels

For LOG footage before LUT conversion, DaVinci also provides Log Wheels (Shadow, Midtone, Highlight, Offset) that correspond to the LOG tonal mapping rather than the output tonal range. When working with the color managed pipeline, DaVinci automatically applies the correct wheels for the material.

sub_units.title: Primary Color Correction: Wheels, Curves & Scopes
sub_units.description: Using DaVinci Resolve's primary color correction tools methodically: reading and interpreting scopes, setting exposure with the Lift/Gamma/Gain wheels, and using Curves for precise tonal and color control.
sub_units.text_content:
Primary color correction is applied to the entire image simultaneously — no masks or qualifiers. It is the foundation layer of every grade, and it must be correct before any creative work begins. The key to accurate primary correction is using the scopes rather than trusting your eyes on a potentially uncalibrated monitor.

### Video Scopes in DaVinci Resolve

Open scopes: Workspace → Scopes → Select scope type. The four key scopes:

**Waveform:**
* Displays luminance (brightness) vertically: 0% (black) at the bottom, 100% (white) at the top
* Horizontal position in the waveform corresponds to horizontal position in the image
* Used to: set black point (shadows touching 0), set white point (highlights near 100 without clipping), assess overall exposure
* Target: Shadows resting at 5-10% (not crushed to 0), highlights at 90-95% (not blown past 100)

**Parade (RGB Parade):**
* Shows three side-by-side waveforms for the Red, Green, and Blue channels
* If the parade channels are unequal, the image has a color cast (e.g., Blue higher than Red = blue/cool cast)
* Used to: correct white balance, neutralize color casts. For a neutral gray area (like a concrete building or an overcast sky), all three channels should align at the same level

**Vectorscope:**
* Shows color saturation and hue in a circular display
* A perfectly neutral gray image has a dot at the center
* The direction from center indicates hue; distance from center indicates saturation
* Skin tones fall along a specific line in the vectorscope (the 'skin tone line') — a quick reference for evaluating portrait material
* Used to: assess saturation levels, confirm color cast removal, target specific color adjustments

**Histogram:**
* Shows the distribution of all tonal values from black (left) to white (right)
* Identical to camera histogram — peaks at the extremes indicate clipping
* Use to confirm no clipping in highlights or shadows after correction

### Curves for Precise Control

The Curves tool in DaVinci Resolve provides more precise tonal control than the wheels:

**Custom Curves (Luma Curve):**
* A diagonal line from bottom-left (black) to top-right (white)
* Click anywhere on the line to add a control point; drag it up to brighten, down to darken
* S-curve (raising midtones while constraining shadows and highlights) adds contrast while preserving detail
* Used for: precise contrast control, creative tone mapping

**Hue vs. Saturation Curve:**
* Adjust the saturation of any specific hue range independently
* Example: Reduce the saturation of only the blue channel (sky) without affecting greens or oranges

**Hue vs. Luminance Curve:**
* Brighten or darken specific hue ranges
* Example: Brighten greens to make vegetation pop without affecting the sky

**Hue vs. Hue Curve:**
* Shift the hue of a specific color range to another hue
* Example: Push teal towards blue for a cooler sky; push orange towards red for a warmer sunset

**Luminance vs. Saturation Curve:**
* Reduce saturation in highlights and shadows while preserving saturation in midtones
* Prevents harsh highlight and shadow color from fighting the grade

sub_units.title: Secondary Corrections: Qualifiers, Masks & Sky Replacement
sub_units.description: Using DaVinci Resolve's secondary tools — HSL Qualifier, Power Windows, and tracking — to make targeted adjustments to specific parts of the image without affecting the rest.
sub_units.text_content:
Primary corrections apply to the entire image. Secondary corrections target specific parts of the image — a specific color range, a specific region of the frame — allowing independent adjustments that would otherwise be impossible. This is where grades become sophisticated and polished.

### HSL Qualifier

The HSL (Hue-Saturation-Luminance) Qualifier is a tool that creates a selection based on color attributes in the image. It is used to isolate a specific color range for independent adjustment.

**Example: Grading the sky independently from the ground**

1. Add a new node (serial or parallel) after your primary correction node
2. Open the Qualifier panel (the eyedropper icon)
3. Use the eyedropper to click on the sky in the image — the qualifier selects the sky's hue, saturation, and luminance ranges
4. Refine the selection using the HSL sliders until only the sky is selected (shown in the Qualifier Matte view: white = selected, black = not selected)
5. Soften the selection edges with the 'Blur Radius' slider to avoid harsh, visible masking
6. Now adjust color wheels or curves — changes apply only to the qualified (sky) portion of the image
7. Press Highlight to toggle the matte view on and off while working

**Common qualifier uses:**
* Making sky more blue and dramatic without affecting green vegetation
* Correcting different-colored grass areas within a single clip
* Reducing saturation of overly vibrant highlights
* Selectively boosting or reducing shadows in specific color areas

### Power Windows (Masks)

Power Windows are geometric or custom-drawn masks that constrain color adjustments to specific regions of the frame:

* **Linear (gradient):** A linear gradient from fully selected to not selected. Used to darken/brighten the sky vs. the ground — equivalent to a graduated ND filter applied in post.
* **Circular / Oval:** Selects an elliptical region. Useful for vignettes.
* **Bezier / Freeform:** Draw a custom mask shape around any area. Used for precise subject isolation.
* **Magic Mask (DaVinci Studio):** AI-powered subject isolation — DaVinci auto-detects and masks a subject across the clip without manual keyframing.

**Sky gradient mask workflow:**
1. Add a new node after primary correction
2. Open Power Windows → select Linear
3. Position the gradient to cover the sky area (above horizon line)
4. Invert the mask if needed (so sky = selected)
5. Adjust the sky: push it slightly cooler and more saturated; add contrast in the sky node
6. The horizon area gets a blend transition between sky and ground adjustment

### Tracking

For shots with camera movement, masks and qualifiers need to track the moving image or they will drift off the intended subject.

In DaVinci Resolve:
1. After creating a qualifier or Power Window, open the Tracker panel
2. Click 'Track Forward' — DaVinci automatically tracks the selected region or color through the clip's duration
3. Review the tracking path — correct any frames where tracking was lost by manually repositioning the mask keyframe

**GPU acceleration:** Tracking is compute-intensive. A modern GPU dramatically speeds up the process.

sub_units.title: Building a Cinematic Look
sub_units.description: Developing a consistent, professional visual look for aerial footage: contrast shaping, the orange-teal grade, complementary color harmony, creating a look LUT, and applying grades consistently across a project.
sub_units.text_content:
A 'look' in cinematography is a consistent, recognizable visual style applied across a body of work or a single piece. Developing a signature look — or matching a client's brand palette — is a professional skill that distinguishes polished productions from mere technical exercises.

### The Classic Cinematic Contrast Shape

Most professional film and television color grades share a common contrast shape:
* Shadows are not fully crushed to pure black — they are slightly 'lifted' (gray-ish). This is often called 'lifted blacks' or 'milky blacks.' It creates a softer, more film-like feel.
* Midtones are full and present — not pushed bright or dark
* Highlights are gently compressed — not harsh, with detail preserved in brighter areas
* The result is a 'soft S-curve' on the luma curve — subtle contrast increase in the midtones with rolloff in the extremes

**Implementation in Resolve:**
* Luma Curve: Raise the bottom of the curve slightly from pure 0% to about 5% (lifts the blacks)
* Pull the top down slightly from 100% to about 95% (compresses highlights)
* Create a subtle S in the midrange

### The Orange-Teal Grade

The most widely used cinematic color grade in contemporary film is the orange-teal (or orange-cyan) grade. It works by:
* Pushing the shadows toward teal/cyan
* Pushing the highlights (and midtones) toward orange/warm tones
* These two colors are complementary (opposite on the color wheel) — they naturally create visual tension and separation

**Why it is so prevalent:** Human skin tones are predominantly orange/warm. By pushing shadows cool (teal), the warm skin tones separate naturally from the darker background areas. It creates a pleasing, subconscious contrast between the subject and the environment.

**Implementation in Resolve:**
* Lift (shadows) wheel: Push slightly toward Cyan
* Gain (highlights) wheel: Push slightly toward Orange/Warm
* Adjust the strength to taste — subtlety is key; heavy-handed orange-teal grading is a cliché

### Saturation Control

**Global saturation:** Saturation slider in the Color Wheels panel. Small adjustments (±10-20%) have a significant visual effect. For a cinematic look, saturation is typically desaturated slightly from the camera default.

**Selective saturation:** Using the Hue vs. Saturation curve to boost specific colors (make the sky more vivid, make vegetation greener) while desaturating others (reduce orange in the skin; reduce the neon harshness of certain LED light sources).

**Protecting skin tones:** In any grade with people, set the vectorscope visible and ensure skin tones stay on or near the skin tone line. Grades that inadvertently push skin tones green or red are immediately noticed by audiences.

### Creating and Exporting a LUT for Reuse

Once you develop a grade you want to reuse across projects or clips:
1. In DaVinci Resolve, right-click on the grade thumbnail in the Gallery → 'Export LUT'
2. Export as .cube (3D LUT) with a descriptive name (e.g., 'DJI-DLogM-Orange-Teal-Cinematic.cube')
3. Import the LUT into any future project and apply it as a starting point on new clips
4. Fine-tune clip by clip after applying the base LUT

**Building a LUT library:** Maintain a personal LUT library organized by camera, LOG format, and look style. This dramatically speeds up the start of every new grading session.

### Clip Matching

For a sequence of clips shot at different times or light conditions, use DaVinci Resolve's shot matching tools:
* **Stills gallery comparison:** Grade one clip to your standard, then use it as a reference while grading adjacent clips. Use the split-screen wipe to compare clips.
* **Auto Color Match (Resolve 18+):** Select a reference clip and target clip → right-click → 'Color Match.' DaVinci analyzes both clips and applies an automatic correction to match them. Manual refinement is always needed after.
* **Parade scope matching:** The most reliable manual method — make the RGB Parade values of the target clip match the reference clip's values in corresponding areas (like the sky or a concrete surface visible in both clips).

units.title: Advanced Editing: Cuts, Transitions, Motion & Audio
units.sub_title: Elevating the Edit from Assembly to Art
units.description: Advanced cutting techniques (J-cut, L-cut, match cut), transitions designed for aerial footage, speed ramping and slow motion workflows, music selection and audio design, and adding professional titles and lower thirds.
units.text_content: A rough cut turns footage into a sequence. An advanced edit turns a sequence into an experience. This unit covers the techniques that create the emotional and rhythmic impact of professional aerial media — the cuts that feel inevitable, the transitions that feel organic, the music that pulls the viewer through the piece, and the sound design that makes aerial footage feel alive.

sub_units.title: Advanced Cut Techniques
sub_units.description: J-cut, L-cut, match cut, action cut, and cross-cut — the advanced editing techniques that create seamless, purposeful transitions in aerial sequences.
sub_units.text_content:
Advanced cutting techniques are tools that allow the editor to create invisible transitions between shots — the viewer feels a continuous flow rather than a series of separate clips stitched together.

### The Hard Cut

The instantaneous change from one clip to the next. When executed on a visual or audio beat, the hard cut is the most powerful editing tool available. Rules for a good hard cut:
* Cut on movement: a cut made when subject or camera is in motion is less visible than a cut to a static frame
* Cut on blinks or partial frame obscurement
* Cut on an audio beat (music hit, dialogue, sound effect)
* Match the visual energy between outgoing and incoming shots

### J-Cut

**Definition:** The audio from the incoming (next) clip begins before the picture transition. The viewer hears what is coming before they see it.

**Why it works:** The brain processes the incoming audio, builds expectation, and the picture cut feels anticipated rather than abrupt. Used extensively in documentary and narrative films when moving between scenes.

**For aerial drone reels:** If a new music section begins 1 second before the cut to a dramatic reveal, the viewer's emotional preparation for the new image is established. The music starts; the image follows.

**How to create in DaVinci Resolve:** Extend the audio track of the incoming clip leftward (earlier in time) past the picture cut point, while keeping the video track at its original cut point.

### L-Cut

**Definition:** The audio from the outgoing (current) clip continues after the picture has already cut to the next clip. You are seeing the new scene while still hearing the old one.

**Why it works:** The lingering audio from the previous scene creates a sense of emotional continuity even as the picture changes. Common in dialogue scenes (see the interviewer while hearing the interviewee's answer).

**For aerial reels:** Allow ambient sound or narration from one scene to overlap into the beginning of an aerial sequence before the new scene's audio takes over.

### Match Cut

**Definition:** A cut between two shots where a visual element (shape, movement, direction, color) precisely matches between the outgoing and incoming frame.

**Examples:**
* An aerial wide shot of a circular fountain cuts to an aerial shot of a circular stadium — the circle matches
* A drone rising to reveal a road cuts to a ground-level shot of the same road — the road direction and composition match
* A spiraling camera movement cuts to a similar spiral movement on a different subject

**Why it works:** The visual rhyme creates a satisfying sense of continuity and draws a visual or thematic connection between the two subjects.

**In practice:** Train yourself to notice potential match-cut opportunities while reviewing footage. A great match cut is often spotted in the selects review, not planned in advance.

### Action Cut

**Definition:** A cut made during a continuous movement, where the action continues across the cut from a different camera angle.

**Example:** A drone approaching a cliff edge; cut at the moment the drone crosses the edge to a reverse angle showing the drop — the approach movement continues through the cut.

**For drone editing:** Works particularly well for fly-throughs (cut mid-pass from one angle to another), chase shots (cut from close to wide in mid-pursuit), and reveal shots (cut from the drone's perspective to a ground-level reverse to show what was revealed).

### Cross-Cut (Parallel Edit)

**Definition:** Alternating between two scenes happening simultaneously in different locations, implying a relationship between them.

**For aerial + ground cutting:** Alternating between aerial shots of a location and ground-level shots of a person in the same location creates both context (aerial shows scale and environment) and intimacy (ground shows the human detail). The cross-cut rhythm drives the viewer's engagement.

sub_units.title: Transitions for Aerial Footage
sub_units.description: Designing and applying transitions that complement aerial footage: whip pan, dip to black, motion-based transitions, and the principles that determine when and how to use them.
sub_units.text_content:
Transitions are visual bridges between shots or scenes. Unlike basic hard cuts, transitions involve a visual or audio effect. The cardinal rule: every transition should serve a storytelling purpose. A transition that exists merely to 'be fancy' feels amateurish. A transition that naturally emerges from the footage's own motion feels invisible and professional.

### When to Use a Transition vs. Hard Cut

**Use hard cuts:** Within a sequence where continuity of place or time is implied; between similarly energized shots; whenever a direct edit serves the story

**Use transitions:**
* To mark a change in location, time period, or emotional register
* To connect stylistically distinct segments
* When a hard cut would be too jarring and the story requires a softer bridge

**Avoid dissolves between aerial shots** unless the sequence is intentionally dreamlike. Dissolves between aerial footage often look muddy and slow — the moving sky and landscape create a confusing double-exposure mid-dissolve.

### Motion-Based Transitions (Most Useful for Aerial)

**Whip Pan / Whip Zoom:**
At the end of the outgoing clip, the image blurs from a fast yaw or zoom. The incoming clip begins with a matching blur that resolves into the new image. The blur masks the cut entirely.
* Create naturally by adding a fast yaw at the end of a drone movement
* Can be added in post using a 'Directional Blur' effect (Premiere: Video Effects → Blur; Resolve: OpenFX → Blur)
* Works best for fast-paced, high-energy edits

**Movement-Match Transition:**
Outgoing clip ends with the camera/drone moving in a specific direction (ascending, moving right). Incoming clip begins with the same movement direction and velocity. The viewer perceives continuous movement even though the location has changed.
* The most elegant aerial transition — requires planning during the shoot
* Shoot exit movements and entry movements that match for clips you plan to bridge

**Spin Transition:**
Drone yaws rapidly at the end/beginning of clips. Two clips connected where both spin in the same direction appear to be one continuous rotation.

### The Dip to Black / White / Color

A brief dip (2-4 frames) to pure black or white at the cut point. Shorter than a full fade — creates a flash-cut feel rather than a scene-break feel.

**For aerial:** Dip-to-black works well at structural breaks: moving from golden-hour establishing shots to mid-day activity shots implies a time passage. Dip-to-white works for sequences that want a bright, airy, high-key feel.

### Zoom Transition

Outgoing clip ends with a digital zoom into a point in the image. Incoming clip begins zoomed in to a matching area and then zooms out. Creates a sense of diving into and emerging from the image.

**Implementation in DaVinci Resolve / Premiere:**
* Add a Transform node (Resolve) or Scale effect (Premiere) to both clips
* Keyframe the scale: on the outgoing clip, scale from 100% to ~120% over 15-20 frames; on the incoming clip, scale from ~120% to 100%
* Ease the keyframes for a smooth acceleration/deceleration (Bezier handles in Resolve, Ease In/Out in Premiere)

### Transition Principles

1. **Subtlety first:** The best transitions are barely noticed. If the viewer notices the transition technique rather than the story, it has failed.
2. **Match energy:** A high-energy whip transition cutting into a slow contemplative shot creates dissonance.
3. **Consistency within a piece:** Use the same transition style throughout a piece — or reserve a specific transition for a specific purpose (e.g., whip pan only for sports sequences).
4. **Limit variety:** Using 4+ different transition types in a single piece looks like a software demo. Choose 1-2 and use them purposefully.

sub_units.title: Speed Ramping & Slow Motion Workflows
sub_units.description: Using high frame rate drone footage for cinematic slow motion, speed ramping between normal and slow motion, and the technical requirements for achieving smooth speed changes.
sub_units.text_content:
Speed manipulation is one of the most powerful tools available to the aerial editor. Slowing down a fast drone movement dramatically increases its emotional weight; a full-speed approach followed by a ramped slow-motion reveal is one of the most effective cinematic techniques in contemporary aerial videography.

### High Frame Rate Capture for Slow Motion

**Technical requirement:** To create slow motion, you need more source frames than the playback frame rate. If your project is 24fps and you want 4x slow motion, you need footage captured at 96fps (24 × 4). The table shows what frame rate is needed for common slow-motion ratios:

| Source FPS | Playback FPS | Slow Motion Factor |
|---|---|---|
| 30fps | 24fps | 1.25x |
| 60fps | 24fps | 2.5x |
| 60fps | 30fps | 2x |
| 120fps | 24fps | 5x |
| 120fps | 30fps | 4x |
| 240fps | 24fps | 10x |

**Quality trade-off at high frame rates:** Most drone cameras reduce resolution or bit rate at high frame rates. The DJI Mavic 3 Pro records 5.1K at 50fps but only 4K at 120fps. The DJI Mini 4 Pro records 1080p at 100fps. Plan what resolution you need for the delivery format before deciding on frame rate.

### Creating Slow Motion in DaVinci Resolve

**Method 1: Clip Retime**
1. Right-click a clip on the timeline → 'Change Clip Speed'
2. Enter the slow-motion percentage: 100% = normal; 40% = 2.5x slow (for 60fps at 24fps playback); 20% = 5x slow (for 120fps at 24fps playback)
3. Check 'Ripple Timeline' if you want the clip to lengthen on the timeline; uncheck if you want to fill a fixed duration

**Method 2: Retime Controls (for variable speed)**
1. Select the clip → Retime Controls (Cmd+R / Ctrl+R)
2. The Retime Speed bar appears at the bottom of the clip
3. Click 'Add Retime Speed Point' at the point where you want speed to change
4. Drag the speed indicator on each segment to set its speed independently

### Speed Ramping: The Ramp Technique

Speed ramping is a gradual acceleration or deceleration from one speed to another, rather than an abrupt speed change. The transition between speeds must be smooth (ease in/out) or it appears jarring.

**Classic aerial speed ramp technique:**
* Footage starts at full speed (100%) — an approach or reveal
* At the dramatic moment (an apex, a reveal, a movement peak), ramp down to 25-40% slow motion
* At the end of the shot, ramp back up to 100% as the clip exits

**In DaVinci Resolve:**
1. Add Retime Speed Points at the start and end of the slow-motion segment
2. Right-click each speed point → 'Change Speed Ramp' → select 'Ease In' (for deceleration) or 'Ease Out' (for acceleration)
3. The easing curve is displayed — adjust the curve handles for the duration and curvature of the ramp

**In Premiere Pro:**
1. Right-click clip → 'Show Clip Keyframes' → 'Time Remapping' → 'Speed'
2. The speed keyframe band appears above the clip's audio waveform
3. Cmd-click on the band to add keyframes; drag them to set speeds
4. Drag the keyframe handles to create a bezier curve (smooth ramp)

### Optical Flow Frame Blending

When footage is slowed beyond its native frame rate (e.g., 30fps footage slowed to 25%), the software must generate intermediate frames. The quality of this generation significantly affects the result:

* **Nearest (Frame Duplicate):** Simply repeats frames. Looks choppy. Unacceptable for visible slow motion.
* **Frame Blend:** Blends adjacent frames. Creates a ghosting/motion blur effect. Acceptable for subtle slow-motion.
* **Optical Flow:** AI-analyzes motion vectors in adjacent frames and generates genuine intermediate frames. Produces smooth results for gentle to moderate slow motion. Can create artifacts with very fast or complex motion.

**Recommendation:** Use Optical Flow for any slow motion below about 50% of the source frame rate. Accept Nearest/Frame Blend for extreme slow motion where Optical Flow artifacts become worse than the duplication.

sub_units.title: Music Selection & Audio Design for Drone Reels
sub_units.description: Choosing music that elevates aerial footage, licensing music legally for commercial use, audio mixing fundamentals, adding ambient sound, and layering audio for professional polish.
sub_units.text_content:
Music is the emotional engine of a drone reel. The same footage set to slow, melancholic piano feels completely different than the same footage set to driving orchestral strings or upbeat electronic music. Audio is not a secondary concern — it is inseparable from the visual experience.

### Music Selection Principles

**Match energy to content:** Contemplative landscape footage demands music with space and breathing room. Action sports footage demands driving, forward-moving music. Real estate and corporate footage typically uses positive, neutral background music that supports without demanding attention.

**Instrumentation and color:** Different instruments carry different emotional associations:
* Piano: Intimate, melancholic, personal
* Strings: Epic, emotional, sweeping
* Electronic/synth: Modern, energetic, cinematic (Hans Zimmer style)
* Acoustic guitar: Warm, rustic, approachable
* Percussion-heavy: Urgency, action, power

**BPM (Beats Per Minute):** The music's tempo determines the natural cut rhythm. Cut to the beat. For high-energy edits, a BPM of 120-140 allows fast cuts. For contemplative edits, 60-80 BPM allows long, breathing shots.

**Arc matching:** Music typically has a structural arc — intro → build → climax → resolution. Align your visual story arc with the music's arc. The big reveal should hit at the music's climax; the quiet establishing shots should be in the intro. This alignment creates a feeling of inevitability and emotional satisfaction.

### Music Licensing for Commercial Work

**Copyright law:** Using commercial music without a license in any video for commercial purposes is copyright infringement. This applies to YouTube videos monetized by ads, client deliverables, and any published content.

**Royalty-free music libraries:**
* **Artlist.io:** Annual subscription ($200/year) with commercial license for all uses including client work. Professional quality catalog.
* **Musicbed:** Premium subscription with broad commercial licenses. High production quality.
* **Epidemic Sound:** Monthly subscription ($15-$99/month). Good quality, personal and commercial tiers.
* **Storyblocks:** Annual subscription with large library. Variable quality.
* **YouTube Audio Library:** Free, commercial use allowed for YouTube. Limited selection.

**Sync licensing for specific tracks:** If a client requires a specific well-known song, a sync license must be negotiated directly with the publisher. Costs range from hundreds to hundreds of thousands of dollars depending on the song and usage.

### Audio Mixing Basics

**Track hierarchy:**
* Music: Primary audio track. Mix to approximately -18 to -12 dBFS (decibels relative to full scale) — not too loud, not background-level
* Narration/dialogue: Peaks at approximately -6 to -3 dBFS — clearly intelligible above the music
* Ambient sound / SFX: Fill out the soundscape without competing with music or dialogue

**Levels and Loudness:**
For video delivery, the target is Integrated Loudness of approximately -14 LUFS for YouTube and streaming platforms, and -23 LUFS for broadcast. DaVinci Resolve's Fairlight page and Premiere Pro's Audio panel both include loudness meters.

**Ducking:** When narration or dialogue is present, automatically lower the music level underneath it. In Resolve: use a keyframe on the audio clip's gain to lower it at dialogue entry and restore it after. In Premiere: use the 'Auto Ducking' feature via Essential Sound panel.

### Ambient Sound for Aerial Footage

Pure music-only drone reels can feel sterile. Adding ambient sound — wind, birds, distant traffic, ocean waves — grounds the footage in a real physical environment and creates a sense of presence.

**Sources:**
* Record ambient sound on-site with a directional microphone or smartphone
* Royalty-free ambient sound libraries (Freesound.org, Epidemic Sound SFX)

**Mixing ambient sound:** Blend ambient sound low beneath the music (6-12 dB below music level). Allow it to breathe through quiet moments in the music. Subtle ambient sound is almost more effective than prominent ambient sound — it subliminally adds realism without the viewer consciously noticing it.

sub_units.title: Titles, Lower Thirds & Motion Graphics
sub_units.description: Designing and animating professional titles, location titles, and lower thirds using DaVinci Resolve's Fusion and Premiere Pro's Essential Graphics, and when to use motion graphics vs. clean simplicity.
sub_units.text_content:
Text and graphics communicate information that the images cannot — location names, subject identification, brand identity, and context. When designed well, they reinforce the visual style of the piece. When designed poorly, they undermine it immediately.

### Principles of Text in Video

**Legibility first:** Text must be readable at the intended display size, in motion, and over a variety of background images (dark backgrounds, bright backgrounds, busy textures). Test text over the most challenging background in your footage.

**White text with shadow:** A white or light-colored text with a subtle drop shadow or soft text stroke is legible over almost any background. Black text works over bright backgrounds. Avoid low-contrast text color choices.

**On-screen duration:** Title cards should remain on screen long enough to be read comfortably: a general rule is 1 second per 5-6 words for flowing reading. For short location titles (2-4 words), 2-3 seconds is typically sufficient. Do not rush titles.

**Typography:** Use one font family with two weights (e.g., regular for descriptive text, bold for title text) rather than multiple unrelated fonts. Clean, modern sans-serif fonts work for contemporary aerial reels; serif fonts work for more classical or documentary styles.

### Title Cards

**Opening title card:** Sets tone and branding. Typically appears at the beginning of a piece after an attention-grabbing opening shot, not before the footage starts.
* Background: full-screen color, a still frame from the piece, or an animation
* Duration: 2-5 seconds for a short reel; up to 8 seconds for longer pieces

**Location title:** Appears briefly over the footage to identify a location. Should be unobtrusive — bottom left or lower center of the frame, fading in and out with a dissolve or upward slide animation.

### Lower Thirds

A lower third is a graphic that occupies the lower third of the frame and typically identifies a person, location, or product. For real-estate and event drone work, lower thirds appear over establishing aerial shots to identify the property or location.

**Standard lower-third format:**
* Line 1: Primary information (person's name, property address, event name) — larger, bolder text
* Line 2: Secondary information (title, neighborhood, price) — smaller, lighter text
* Optional graphic element: A colored bar, line, or box behind the text for legibility

**Animation:** Standard lower-third animations include:
* Slide in from left, slide out to left (L-cut style)
* Fade in from transparent, fade out to transparent
* Wipe from left edge (drawing the bar on-screen)

### Creating Titles in DaVinci Resolve

1. **Text+ Title in the Edit page:** Effects Library → Titles → Text+. Drag to an empty video track above V1.
2. Open Inspector panel: set font, size, color, position, and tracking
3. Fusion page for animation: click the Fusion composition to open in Fusion; animate text or backing graphic using keyframes
4. Use the 'Smooth In / Smooth Out' built-in modifiers for automatic easing on position and opacity animations

**Using Fusion compositions (templates):** Create a title once in Fusion, save it as a template, and reuse it across the project. This ensures consistency and saves time.

### Creating Titles in Premiere Pro

1. **Essential Graphics panel:** Window → Essential Graphics → Browse tab for built-in templates
2. Or: File → New → Legacy Title (for more manual control)
3. Drag a Motion Graphics Template (.mogrt) from the Browse tab to the timeline
4. Double-click to open and edit text, colors, and font
5. Export custom templates for reuse across projects

**Adobe After Effects integration:** For advanced motion graphics (animated logo, complex animated text), design in After Effects and use Dynamic Link to place the composition directly into the Premiere Pro timeline without rendering.

units.title: Exporting, Delivery & Building Your Portfolio
units.sub_title: From Final Cut to Client Hands and Beyond
